message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="scott-wlaschin" color="yellow" summary="Full alignment: stalled project styling, calm inbox badge, anxiety-reducing design">
Steve, this is beautifully reasoned. Every design choice traces back to a domain principle. A few notes:

**Stalled projects: danger text + warning border -- agreed.**

Your WCAG contrast analysis is exactly right. The warning color alone fails AA for normal text, so danger for the readable text and warning for the ambient border gives you two visual cues that are both accessible. The inline "Add next action" link completes the pattern -- surface the problem AND the fix in the same component. From the domain: `project.is_stalled()` drives the template conditional, and the link targets `/projects/{id}/actions/new`.

**Inbox badge: calm, not urgent -- perfect.**

Your revised treatment is the correct translation of GTD's philosophy into visual design:
- Count in parentheses, muted gray, no pill shape
- Zero count = no count shown (absence is the signal)
- Threshold escalation to `--color-primary` (blue, not red)

This is a case where the domain semantics directly inform the visual weight. The domain says "inbox items are normal, unprocessed is not an error." The design says "muted count, no alarm." Same principle, different medium.

The threshold escalation to blue at 20+ items is a nice touch. It's a nudge, not a warning. "Mind like water" means the UI doesn't create ripples unnecessarily.

**Your design principle is worth adding to TEAM_AGREEMENTS.md:**

> "The UI should reduce anxiety, not create it."

This is the visual design equivalent of "make illegal states unrepresentable" -- the design makes anxious states unrenderable. I'd support adding this to our design conventions section when we update the agreements for V1.

**One domain-design alignment to highlight for the team:**

The fact that the ONLY urgent visual in the app is a stalled project (no next action) maps precisely to the domain: the only true workflow problem in GTD is a project that has stopped moving. Everything else (inbox items, waiting-for items, someday/maybe items) is the system working as intended. Your visual hierarchy reflects this: stalled = danger, everything else = calm. The domain truth IS the design truth.

Nothing further needed from me on the design side. We're aligned.
</teammate-message>

content.text: Scott and I are fully aligned. His observation about the design principle mapping to domain truth is worth noting.

input.content:
Scott, agreed on all points. Two quick notes:

**Adding the principle to TEAM_AGREEMENTS.md:** I'd support that. When we update the agreements for V1, I'll propose it for the Design Conventions section:

> "The UI should reduce anxiety, not create it. Counts inform, they don't alarm. Color signals action opportunities, not emergencies. The only urgent visual is a genuine workflow problem (stalled project), not normal system state (unprocessed inbox)."

**Your domain-design alignment observation** -- "the only true workflow problem in GTD is a project that has stopped moving, and that's the only urgent visual" -- is exactly the kind of insight that makes domain-driven design and visual design reinforce each other. When the domain model and the visual hierarchy agree on what matters, the user gets a coherent experience without having to think about it.

We're fully aligned. Nothing further needed.
input.summary: Fully aligned, supporting design principle in TEAM_AGREEMENTS

routing.summary: Fully aligned, supporting design principle in TEAM_AGREEMENTS

content.text:
All design conversations are now closed with full alignment across every teammate I've engaged with:

| Teammate | Status | Key Outcome |
|----------|--------|-------------|
| Marty | Approved, one change (5 defaults) | Product scope aligned with design |
| Lea | Fully aligned | CSS architecture, tokens, components |
| Heydon | Fully aligned | Nav, badges, clarify form, a11y patterns |
| Scott | Fully aligned | Domain-design mapping, calm UI principle |
| Team lead | Updated | All decisions communicated |

No open questions remain from the design side. Ready for consensus and first implementation task.
